file.content:
{
  "summary": "Deep multi-dimensional review of PR #30 (Yandex Tracker component management)",
  "agentCount": 30,
  "logs": [
    "Total findings: 26 | kept (confirmed/partial): 26 | refuted: 0"
  ],
  "result": {
    "kept": [
      {
        "dimension": "api",
        "title": "component_get and component_update depend on GET /v3/components/{id}, an endpoint the API docs do not document",
        "severityClaimed": "critical",
        "severityAdjusted": "medium",
        "verdict": "partial",
        "file": "mcp_tracker/tracker/custom/client.py",
        "location": "component_get (lines ~416-427) and component_update (lines ~429-461)",
        "description": "The official Yandex Tracker API reference documents only THREE component operations: GET /v3/components (a single org-wide LIST that returns 'a JSON array of all components created within the organization', 200 OK), POST /v3/components (create), and PATCH /v3/components/<id>?version= (edit). The get-components page explicitly states the path is 'simply GET /v3/components with no queue ID' and 'does not mention a GET /v3/components/{id} endpoint for fetching a single component'. This PR's component_get issues GET v3/components/{component_id}, and component_update calls component_get first (read-then-PATCH) to obtain the version. If the single-component GET does not exist on the API, BOTH component_get and component_update are non-functional — every get returns 404 and every update fails before it ever reaches the PATCH. Tracker sometimes supports undocumented by-id GETs for other entities, so this may happen to work, but the PR ships it as the foundation of two tools without any documented backing. This MUST be confirmed against a live tenant before merge; if it does not work, component_get should be removed/replaced with a queue-scoped list and component_update must obtain the version another way.",
        "evidence": "Client: `async with self._session.get(f\"v3/components/{component_id}\", ...)` for component_get; component_update body: `component = await self.component_get(component_id, auth=auth)` then `params = {\"version\": component.version}`. Docs (get-components): 'The path is simply GET /v3/components with no queue ID required' and 'The documentation does not mention a GET /v3/components/{id} endpoint for fetching a single component. Only this list endpoint and endpoints for creating and editing components are documented.'",
        "recommendation": "Verify GET /v3/components/{id} against a real Tracker org. If it works, document that reliance in a code comment. If it does NOT work: (a) drop component_get or back it with the documented list (filter GET /v3/components by id client-side, or add a queue-scoped list); (b) for component_update, fetch the component from the list to read version, or let the caller pass version explicitly so no single-GET is needed.",
        "verifyReasoning": "The finding's factual premises are CONFIRMED, but its severity (\"critical / non-functional / every get returns 404\") is OVERSTATED and unproven.\n\nCONFIRMED facts:\n1. Code: component_get issues `GET v3/components/{component_id}` (client.py:422-423). component_update is read-then-PATCH: `component = await self.component_get(component_id, auth=auth)` then `params = {\"version\": component.version}` and `PATCH v3/components/{component_id}` (client.py:438-458). So component_update fully depends on the by-id GET succeeding before it ever reaches the PATCH.\n2. Docs (official Yandex Tracker, yandex.ru/support/tracker mirror): the ONLY documented component GET is `GET /v3/components` — an org-wide LIST returning \"a list of all components created within the organization\", no queue id, no per-id variant. POST /v3/components (create) and `PATCH /v3/components/<id>?version=` (edit) are documented. No `GET /v3/components/{id}` appears anywhere; web search for it also found nothing.\n\nWHY ONLY PARTIAL / DOWNGRADED FROM CRITICAL:\n- The finding equates \"undocumented\" with \"returns 404 / non-functional\", then immediately hedges (\"Tracker sometimes supports undocumented by-id GETs, so this may happen to work\") and demands live confirmation. Absence from docs is not proof of a 404. I could not reproduce a 404, and the finding supplies no live-tenant evidence either — so the \"every call fails\" / critical framing is speculation, not demonstrated fact.\n- The PATCH endpoint the tool ultimately targets (`PATCH /v3/components/<id>?version=`) IS documented and correct, so the write path is conformant; only the version-fetch step is via an undocumented route.\n- A fully documented mitigation exists and is cheap: the LIST response (GET /v3/components) includes both `id` and `version` for every component (docs confirm \"id\": 1, \"version\": 3 in the example), so component_get could be backed by the list filtered client-side and component_update could read version from there with zero reliance on a by-id GET. The code even guards `if component.version is None: raise ValueError(...)` (client.py:439-442), showing the author anticipated version-absence but not endpoint-absence.\n\nHonest severity: medium. This is a real API-conformance/robustness risk — two tools founded on an undocumented endpoint with no fallback — but the consequence (\"non-functional\") is unverified and likely false given Tracker's conventions and the documented single-resource PATCH shape. It is not a confirmed critical bug. Recommendation stands in spirit: verify GET /v3/components/{id} against a live org; if unreliable, back component_get with the documented list and have component_update read version from the list (or accept an explicit version param)."
      },
      {
        "dimension": "api",
        "title": "component_delete uses DELETE /v3/components/{id}, which has no documented API endpoint",
        "severityClaimed": "high",
        "severityAdjusted": "low",
        "verdict": "partial",
        "file": "mcp_tracker/tracker/custom/client.py",
        "location": "component_delete (lines ~463-474)",
        "description": "The component_delete method issues DELETE v3/components/{component_id}. None of the Yandex Tracker API reference pages document a DELETE endpoint for components — the get-components/post-component pages list only 'get list', 'create', and 'edit' operations, and the user-guide page (manager/components) states deletion is performed only through the UI ('Hover over the component and select delete'). Like the single-GET, Tracker may support this undocumented, but it is unverified. The test only asserts the client sends DELETE and parses a 204 — it does not prove the endpoint exists. Shipping a write tool on an undocumented destructive endpoint is risky.",
        "evidence": "Client: `async with self._session.delete(f\"v3/components/{component_id}\", ...)`. Docs (get-components page): 'The documentation does not mention a DELETE method for components. The page only lists three component operations: Get list, Create, and Edit — no deletion endpoint is documented.' Docs (manager/components): 'Only the UI method is documented... Hover over the component and select delete.'",
        "recommendation": "Confirm DELETE /v3/components/{id} works against a live org and returns 204. If confirmed, note it in a comment (since it's undocumented). If not, remove the delete tool or surface a clear error.",
        "verifyReasoning": "The factual core of the finding is CONFIRMED, but the \"high\" severity is OVERSTATED — it is a documentation-conformance concern, not a demonstrated bug, so I mark the finding partial and adjust severity to low.\n\nConfirmed facts:\n1. client.py component_delete (diff lines 463-474) does issue `DELETE v3/components/{component_id}` with no special error handling beyond response.raise_for_status().\n2. The Yandex Tracker API docs genuinely document only three component operations — GET (list components, /v3/components), POST (create), PATCH (edit). No DELETE endpoint appears. Verified via WebFetch on both the get-components reference page and the patch-component reference page; both sidebars list only Get list / Create / Edit (+ Access). \n3. The user guide (manager/components) confirms deletion is UI-only: \"Hover the cursor over the component. Select → Delete. Confirm the deletion.\" No API method mentioned.\n4. The test (tests/tracker/custom/components/test_component_delete.py) only mocks a 204 via aioresponses and asserts the client sends DELETE and returns None — it does NOT prove the live endpoint exists. Confirmed.\n\nWhy severity is NOT high (down to low):\n- The finding itself hedges: \"Tracker may support this undocumented, but it is unverified.\" I could independently confirm the endpoint is UNDOCUMENTED, but I could NOT prove it is broken. Undocumented != nonexistent — Yandex Tracker has historically supported undocumented endpoints.\n- Failure mode is benign. If the endpoint does not exist, the API returns 404/405 and response.raise_for_status() propagates a clean exception to the MCP caller. There is no silent failure, no data corruption, and crucially no \"returns-None-only-when-caching\" class of bug (the CachingComponentsProtocol.component_delete just delegates to the original; it does not fabricate a None success). This is the opposite of the critical example in my severity guidance.\n- The codebase already ships several documented DELETE write tools (issue links, comments, worklogs at client.py:447/540/665), so shipping a destructive write tool is an established, accepted pattern. The only novel element is that this particular endpoint is undocumented.\n- The identical undocumented-endpoint concern applies to the read tool component_get in the same PR (single GET /v3/components/{id} is also not in the documented sidebar, which only lists the list-GET). The finding acknowledges this (\"Like the single-GET\"), so component_delete is not uniquely risky.\n\nRecommendation stands as reasonable: verify DELETE /v3/components/{id} against a live org (expecting 204) and, if it works, add a code comment noting it is undocumented; the existing destructive-tool pattern and clean error propagation mean no code change is strictly required. Appropriate as a low-severity / nit review comment, not a high-severity blocker."
      },
      {
        "dimension": "api",
        "title": "component_update never sends assignAuto, although the API documents it as an updatable field",
        "severityClaimed": "high",
        "severityAdjusted": "medium",
        "verdict": "confirmed",
        "file": "mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/component_write.py",
        "location": "component_update tool (lines ~225-252) and TrackerClient.component_update / ComponentsProtocol.component_update",
        "description": "The edit-component (patch-component) docs list four updatable body fields: name, description, lead, and assignAuto. component_create correctly supports assign_auto, but component_update has no assign_auto parameter anywhere in the stack (tool, protocol, client, caching wrapper). A user who creates a component with assignAuto=true cannot turn it off, and vice versa, via the MCP server — there is no way to toggle auto-assignment after creation even though the API fully supports it. This is a real functional gap, not a style nit: it makes the update tool unable to express a documented, commonly-needed mutation.",
        "evidence": "Patch-component docs: updatable body fields are 'name (String), description (String), lead (String), assignAuto (Boolean) — whether to automatically assign the owner as default assignee'. component_create signature includes `assign_auto: bool | None` but component_update signature is only `name, description, lead`. Client component_update body builds only name/description/lead: there is no `if assign_auto is not None: body[\"assignAuto\"] = assign_auto`.",
        "recommendation": "Add assign_auto: bool | None to component_update across tool -> protocol -> client -> caching wrapper, mirroring component_create, and set body['assignAuto'] when provided.",
        "verifyReasoning": "The finding is factually correct on every point, verified independently against both the diff and the live Yandex Tracker API docs.\n\nAPI conformance (re-checked via WebFetch of https://yandex.ru/support/tracker/en/concepts/queues/patch-component): the edit-component PATCH endpoint documents four updatable Request body parameters — name (String), description (String), lead (String), and assignAuto (Boolean: \"Default assignee attribute: true: Assign the owner as the default assignee. false: Don't assign a default assignee.\"). So assignAuto IS a documented, updatable field.\n\nCode-side gap, consistent across ALL FOUR layers (the claim that it is missing \"anywhere in the stack\" is accurate):\n- Tool (mcp/tools/component_write.py): component_update params are component_id, name, description, lead — no assign_auto. component_create DOES expose assign_auto: bool | None.\n- Protocol (tracker/proto/components.py): component_update signature lacks assign_auto; component_create has it.\n- Client (tracker/custom/client.py): component_update builds body with only name/description/lead — there is no `if assign_auto is not None: body[\"assignAuto\"] = assign_auto`. component_create DOES set body[\"assignAuto\"].\n- Caching wrapper (tracker/caching/client.py): CachingComponentsProtocol.component_update omits assign_auto; component_create passes it through.\n\nThe asymmetry is therefore real: a user can set assignAuto at creation time but cannot toggle it afterward via the MCP server, even though the API supports it. This is a genuine functional/feature-completeness gap, not a style nit, and the recommendation (add assign_auto across tool -> protocol -> client -> caching, mirroring component_create) is correct and idiomatic for the codebase.\n\nSeverity adjusted from 'high' to 'medium': this is a missing-feature gap, not a correctness bug. There is no data corruption, silent failure, wrong-result, or security impact — component_update works correctly for the fields it does support. The capability is also partially present (assignAuto can be set on create), so only post-creation toggling is missing. The 'high'/'critical' tier in the review rubric is reserved for things like returns-None-only-when-caching bugs; a documented-but-unimplemented optional update field is a bounded medium-severity completeness gap."
      },
      {
        "dimension": "api",
        "title": "Component model drops documented response fields: queue, lead, assignAuto, self",
        "severityClaimed": "medium",
        "severityAdjusted": "medium",
        "verdict": "confirmed",
        "file": "mcp_tracker/tracker/proto/types/components.py",
        "location": "class Component (lines 4-10)",

MRO/STUB (PR diff, mcp_tracker/tracker/proto/components.py): `ComponentsProtocol(Protocol)` declares `async def component_get(...) -> Component: ...` (Ellipsis body). `ComponentsProtocolWrap(ComponentsProtocol)` defines only `__init__` setting `self._original`. Thus `CachingComponentsProtocol.component_get` resolves up the MRO to `ComponentsProtocol.component_get`, the `...` stub.

WIRING (PR diff, server.py): `components = cache_collection.components(components)` when caching enabled — so `AppContext.components` is a `CachingComponentsProtocol` instance. {
        "dimension": "architecture",
        "title": "Import ordering nit in tests/mcp/conftest.py",
        "severityClaimed": "nit",
        "severityAdjusted": "nit",
        "verdict": "confirmed",
        "file": "tests/mcp/conftest.py",
        "location": "import block (diff line ~556)",
        "description": "The new `from mcp_tracker.tracker.proto.components import ComponentsProtocol` import is inserted after the issues import, breaking the otherwise-alphabetical proto import grouping (fields, issues, components, queues, users). Minor, and `task format`/ruff isort would likely reorder it, but worth a pass to keep diffs clean.",
        "evidence": "conftest.py diff: `from ...proto.issues import IssueProtocol` then `from ...proto.components import ComponentsProtocol` then `from ...proto.queues import QueuesProtocol` — components is out of alphabetical order.",
        "recommendation": "Run `task format` so isort orders the proto imports; place components before fields/issues alphabetically.",
        "verifyReasoning": "Independently reproduced from /tmp/pr30.diff lines 552-558, the conftest.py hunk: the import block becomes `fields`, `issues`, then `+from mcp_tracker.tracker.proto.components import ComponentsProtocol` inserted, then `queues`, `users`. `components` is placed after `issues` but should sort before `fields` alphabetically, so it breaks the first-party import ordering. The finding's quoted evidence matches the diff exactly.

The claim that the convention is alphabetical is corroborated by the other hunks in the SAME PR, which all correctly place `components` first: context.py (diff line 35), server.py (line 52), and custom/client.py (line 361) each insert `from ...proto.components import ...` before the `fields` import. conftest.py is the lone deviation. The recommendation ("place components before fields/issues alphabetically") is correct.

Note: the current on-disk tests/mcp/conftest.py (lines 16-19) does not contain the import at all because the working tree is at HEAD/main, not the PR branch — this does not refute the finding, which reviews the PR diff (the authoritative source for what the PR introduces).

Severity correctly assessed as nit: zero runtime/functional impact (imports resolve regardless of order) and trivially auto-fixable. One refinement — the finding slightly understates the consequence by saying ruff "would likely" reorder it. pyproject.toml selects `"I"` (isort) in ruff lint rules (line 80) and all proto imports are in the same first-party section (section-order at lines 102-108), so an unsorted block triggers rule I001. Taskfile.yml's `ruff-check` (`uv run ruff check .`, line 26) is part of the default `task`/`check` target, so this would actually FAIL CI/`task check` until `task format` (line 32: `ruff check --select I,F401 --fix .`) is run — a hard lint failure, not merely a cosmetic preference. Still a nit because it has no behavioral effect and is auto-resolved by the project's own format task."
      },
      {
        "dimension": "correctness",
        "title": "component_get returns None (never delegates) when caching is enabled",
        "severityClaimed": "critical",
        "severityAdjusted": "high",
        "verdict": "confirmed",
        "file": "mcp_tracker/tracker/caching/client.py",
        "location": "CachingComponentsProtocol (diff lines 298-344); compare CachingQueuesProtocol.queue_get at lines 107-115",
        "description": "When settings.tools_cache_enabled is true, AppContext.components is a CachingComponentsProtocol instance (server.py: `components = cache_collection.components(components)`). CachingComponentsProtocol only defines component_create, component_update, and component_delete — it does NOT define component_get. Its MRO is CachingComponentsProtocol -> ComponentsProtocolWrap -> ComponentsProtocol(Protocol). ComponentsProtocolWrap defines only __init__, so a call to component_get resolves to ComponentsProtocol.component_get, whose body is the Ellipsis stub `...`. An `async def` with an Ellipsis body returns a coroutine that yields None. Therefore, with caching on, the component_get MCP tool returns None instead of a Component, never reaching self._original or the real HTTP client. The tool's declared return type is Component, so FastMCP receives None — either a serialization/validation failure or a silently empty result. Every other caching wrapper (CachingQueuesProtocol.queue_get at lines 107-115, CachingUsersProtocol.user_get at 489-493, etc.) explicitly overrides its read methods to delegate to self._original; this one was forgotten. I reproduced the exact behavior: a wrapper missing the get override returns None (RESULT: None) while the real original returns the data. No caching-layer test exists for components (the PR adds none to tests/tracker/caching/), and test_protocol_conformance only checks isinstance against a runtime_checkable Protocol, which passes by method-name existence and cannot catch this. MCP-tool tests pass because they replace the protocol with an AsyncMock, bypassing the broken wrapper entirely.",
        "evidence": "Reproduction:
  class ComponentsProtocolWrap(ComponentsProtocol):
      def __init__(self, original): self._original = original
  class CachingComponentsProtocol(ComponentsProtocolWrap):
      async def component_create(self): ...   # no component_get override
  await CachingComponentsProtocol(Original()).component_get(111175)  # -> None
MRO: ['CachingComponentsProtocol','ComponentsProtocolWrap','ComponentsProtocol','Protocol','Generic','object']; component_get resolves to ComponentsProtocol.component_get; RESULT: None. The proto stub: `async def component_get(self, component_id: int, *, auth=None) -> Component: ...` (proto/components.py diff lines 504-509).",
        "recommendation": "Add a delegating override to CachingComponentsProtocol, mirroring CachingQueuesProtocol.queue_get: `@cached(**cache_config)\nasync def component_get(self, component_id, *, auth=None) -> Component:\n    return await self._original.component_get(component_id, auth=auth)`. Also add a caching-layer test (e.g. tests/tracker/caching/test_components_protocol.py) that wraps an AsyncMock original and asserts component_get actually calls through to it, since the existing conformance/MCP tests cannot detect a missing delegation.",
        "verifyReasoning": "Every claim in the finding is independently verified against the actual PR diff and reproduced in Python.

STRUCTURE (PR diff, mcp_tracker/tracker/caching/client.py, the new `CachingComponentsProtocol`): defines only `component_create`, `component_update`, `component_delete` — NO `component_get` override. Contrast with siblings that DO delegate their read methods: `CachingQueuesProtocol.queue_get` (caching/client.py:107-115), `CachingUsersProtocol.user_get` (caching/client.py:489-493), `CachingIssuesProtocol.issue_get` (118-122). So the components wrapper is the only one missing its get override.

MRO/STUB (PR diff, mcp_tracker/tracker/proto/components.py): `ComponentsProtocol(Protocol)` declares `async def component_get(...) -> Component: ...` (Ellipsis body). `ComponentsProtocolWrap(ComponentsProtocol)` defines only `__init__` setting `self._original`. Thus `CachingComponentsProtocol.component_get` resolves up the MRO to `ComponentsProtocol.component_get`, the `...` stub.

WIRING (PR diff, server.py): `components = cache_collection.components(components)` when caching enabled — so `AppContext.components` is a `CachingComponentsProtocol` instance. Tool (PR diff, mcp/tools/component_read.py) calls `...components.component_get(component_id, auth=...)` with declared return `-> Component`.

REPRODUCTION: I built the exact class hierarchy and ran it. MRO = ['CachingComponentsProtocol','ComponentsProtocolWrap','ComponentsProtocol','Protocol','Generic','object']; `component_get` resolves to `ComponentsProtocol` and returns None; `component_create` correctly returns the delegated value. An `async def` whose body is `...` returns a coroutine yielding None. So with TOOLS_CACHE_ENABLED=true, component_get always returns None, never touching self._original / the HTTP client.

WHY TESTS MISS IT: MCP tool tests inject AsyncMock(spec=ComponentsProtocol) directly as AppContext.components (tests/mcp/tools/conftest.py + test_component_tools.py set component_get.return_value), bypassing the broken wrapper. The PR adds NO caching-layer test for components (tests/tracker/caching/ has test_queues_protocol/test_users_protocol/etc. but no test_components_protocol). test_protocol_conformance.py (verified by reading it) only does isinstance against a @runtime_checkable Protocol, which is hasattr-based and passes on method-name existence — it cannot detect that an inherited stub returns None. The PR doesn't even add a components conformance entry there.

The finding's description, evidence, recommendation, and reproduction are all accurate. The fix is to add a delegating `component_get` override (with @cached) mirroring queue_get, plus a caching-layer test that asserts the wrapper calls through to self._original.

SEVERITY RE-CALIBRATION: This is a genuine, reproducible functional bug — not a nit. component_get returns None for 100% of calls under caching, and the tool declares -> Component, so FastMCP gets None (broken/empty result). However I downgrade from the claimed 'critical' to 'high' on honest calibration: it is gated behind an opt-in config (TOOLS_CACHE_ENABLED), affects exactly one of the four component tools (the three writes delegate correctly), and the default/non-caching path works fine. It is not data-corrupting and not a default-on/all-paths failure, which is what I reserve 'critical' for. It still fully breaks a supported configuration of a shipped tool, so 'high' is appropriate."
      },
      {
        "dimension": "correctness",
        "title": "Component model silently drops queue, lead, and assignAuto from every API response",
        "severityClaimed": "high",
        "severityAdjusted": "medium",
        "verdict": "confirmed",
        "file": "mcp_tracker/tracker/proto/types/components.py",
        "location": "class Component (diff lines 541-547)",
        "description": "The Component model declares only id, version, name, description. The Yandex Tracker components API returns (and the create/update bodies set) additional fields: queue, lead, and assignAuto. Because Component inherits BaseTrackerEntity with model_config extra="ignore", these fields are silently discarded during model_validate_json. Observable wrong behavior: a user creates or updates a component setting lead="testuser" and assign_auto=True, the tool returns a Component that contains neither the lead nor the assignAuto nor the owning queue. The user cannot see which queue a component belongs to (relevant for the access-control gap below) nor confirm the lead/auto-assign was applied. The PR's own tests demonstrate the loss: tests/tracker/custom/components/test_component_create.py::test_with_lead_and_assign_auto returns a payload containing `lead` and `assignAuto`, but only asserts `result.name == "LeadTest"` — it never asserts the lead/assignAuto survived, because they cannot.",
        "evidence": "Model: `class Component(BaseTrackerEntity):\n    id: int|None ...\n    version: int|None ...\n    name: str|None ...\n    description: str|None ...` (no queue/lead/assignAuto). BaseTrackerEntity: `model_config = ConfigDict(extra="ignore")`. API response in tests includes `"queue": {...}`, `"lead": {...}`, `"assignAuto": True` — all dropped.",
        "recommendation": "Add the missing fields to the Component model so creates/updates round-trip visibly: e.g. `queue: QueueReference | None = NoneExcludedField`, `lead: UserReference | None = NoneExcludedField` (using the existing refs types), and `assign_auto: bool | None = Field(None, alias="assignAuto", ...)`. At minimum add `queue` so the access-control fix below is feasible and so users can see the owning queue.",
        "verifyReasoning": "The finding is factually correct on every claim I could independently reproduce.

1. Model under-specifies fields (CONFIRMED). In the PR diff, mcp_tracker/tracker/proto/types/components.py (lines 541-547) declares the Component model with only id, version, name, description:
  class Component(BaseTrackerEntity):
      id: int | None = NoneExcludedField
      version: int | None = NoneExcludedField
      name: str | None = NoneExcludedField
      description: str | None = NoneExcludedField
No queue, lead, or assignAuto field.

2. extra="ignore" causes silent drop (CONFIRMED). mcp_tracker/tracker/proto/types/base.py line 7-9: BaseTrackerEntity sets model_config = ConfigDict(extra="ignore"). With pydantic, extra="ignore" discards unknown keys during model_validate_json rather than retaining them, so any API field not declared on the model is silently lost.

3. The API actually returns these fields (CONFIRMED against live docs, not just the claim). I fetched the live Yandex Tracker docs for get-components (yandex.ru/support/tracker/ru/concepts/queues/get-components). The documented component object explicitly includes self, id, version, name, queue (self/id/key/display), description, lead (self/id/display/cloudUid/passportUid), and assignAuto (boolean). So queue, lead, and assignAuto are genuinely returned and genuinely dropped by this model. The client methods (custom/client.py in the diff, lines 390-461) all parse responses via Component.model_validate_json, so the loss occurs on create, get, and update.

4. The PR's own test demonstrates the loss (CONFIRMED). tests/tracker/custom/components/test_component_create.py::test_with_lead_and_assign_auto (diff lines 940-986) builds a response payload containing lead and assignAuto: True, calls component_create(lead="testuser", assign_auto=True), but then asserts only result.name == "LeadTest" plus the request body. It never asserts result.lead or result.assign_auto survived — consistent with them being undeclared and dropped.

Severity reassessment (claimed high -> I set medium). The write operation itself is NOT broken: component_create correctly puts name/queue/description/lead/assignAuto into the request body (diff lines 400-406), and the request-body assertions in the tests pass, so the server-side change is actually applied. The defect is purely an observability/round-trip gap: the returned Component object cannot confirm the lead/assignAuto/owning-queue that the user just set. This is a real user-facing correctness issue (the returned model is incomplete) but it does not cause wrong writes, data corruption, or a silently-failing operation — unlike the high/critical class of returns-None-only-when-caching bugs. Hence medium rather than high. The recommendation (add queue, lead via existing UserReference/QueueReference refs types, and assign_auto with alias="assignAuto") is sound and matches the codebase convention (refs.py already defines QueueReference and UserReference; the Queue model in queues.py uses the same NoneExcludedField pattern). The supporting note that adding queue would enable an access-control fix is plausible but belongs to the separate access-control finding; it does not affect the validity of this one."
      },
      {
        "dimension": "correctness",
        "title": "component_update / component_delete / component_get bypass TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES access control",
        "severityClaimed": "high",
        "severityAdjusted": "high",
        "verdict": "confirmed",
        "file": "mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/component_write.py",
        "location": "component_update (diff lines 225-252), component_delete (diff lines 259-269), and component_read.py component_get (diff lines 145-155)",
        "description": "component_create correctly calls check_queue_access(settings, queue_id) before writing. But component_update, component_delete, and component_get take only an integer component_id and perform NO access check. When TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES is configured to restrict access to a subset of queues, a caller can read, modify, or delete a component belonging to a restricted queue simply by passing its numeric id, since the id does not encode the queue and is never validated. This is the same class of restriction the codebase enforces everywhere else (check_issue_access / check_queue_access). Notably component_update already performs an internal GET (client.component_update calls self.component_get) that retrieves the component's queue, so the information needed to enforce access is available but unused — and component_get's restriction-bypass is also not covered by any test (the PR's restriction test only exercises component_create with client_session_with_limits).",
        "evidence": "component_create: `check_queue_access(settings, queue_id)` then create. component_update: body goes straight to `...components.component_update(component_id, ...)` with no check. component_delete: straight to `...component_delete(component_id, ...)`. component_get (read tool): straight to `...component_get(component_id, ...)`. _access.check_queue_access raises TrackerError when `queue_id not in settings.tracker_limit_queues`.",
        "recommendation": "Enforce access on all three: have the write tools (and read tool) resolve the component's queue via component_get and call check_queue_access on the returned queue key before performing the mutation/return; or require the queue_id as a parameter and validate it. Add restriction tests using client_session_with_limits for component_get, component_update, and component_delete (mirroring the existing component_create restriction test).",
        "verifyReasoning": "The core access-control bypass is real and independently reproduced from the branch source (component-management) plus live Yandex Tracker API docs.

CODE (verified on branch component-management):
- mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/component_write.py: component_create calls `check_queue_access(settings, queue_id)` before mutating (line matching diff 210), exactly mirroring the queue_create_version precedent in queue_write.py:44. But component_update and component_delete take only `component_id: int` and call the protocol method directly with NO check.
- mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/component_read.py: component_get likewise takes only `component_id: int` and calls the protocol directly with no check.
- mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/_access.py:15-18: check_queue_access raises TrackerError when `settings.tracker_limit_queues and queue_id not in settings.tracker_limit_queues` — i.e., this is the codebase's queue allow-list boundary, applied everywhere else (check_issue_access / check_queue_access).

API CONFORMANCE (verified via live docs, yandex.ru/support/tracker):
- Update endpoint: `PATCH /v3/components/<id_компонента>?version=...` — keyed purely by numeric component id; the queue does NOT appear in the path. The GET (`/v3/components`) and the client's `f"v3/components/{component_id}"` for GET/PATCH/DELETE confirm the same pattern. Because the numeric id does not encode the queue and is never validated, a caller with TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES configured can read/modify/delete a component belonging to a restricted queue.

No restriction tests exist for component_get, component_update, or component_delete. Confirmed.

That is only half-true. The raw HTTP GET response does contain a `queue` object (confirmed in docs), but the parsed Component model (proto/types/components.py) only declares id/version/name/description, and BaseTrackerEntity uses ConfigDict(extra="ignore"), so the `queue` field is silently dropped from the Python object. So enforcing access via the existing GET is not possible as-is; the fix would require adding a `queue` field to the Component model (or requiring queue_id as a tool parameter). This is a flaw in the finding's suggested mechanism, not in the vulnerability claim itself.

SEVERITY: high is correct. It is a genuine, destructive (delete is irreversible) access-control bypass of a configured security boundary, matching the severity of every other queue-restriction in the codebase. Not critical because it only has effect when TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES is configured and it requires knowing a numeric component id (enumerable, but not the open-default case). Not lower because it silently defeats an explicitly-configured authorization restriction across read, write, and delete.

RECOMMENDATION (refined): Add a `queue` field to the Component model and have component_get/update/delete resolve the component's queue via component_get then call check_queue_access on the returned queue key; or require queue_id as an explicit tool parameter and validate it. Add client_session_with_limits restriction tests for all three tools mirroring the existing component_create test."

The finding is filed under "tests," and the honest conclusion is that the tests are fine; the gap is implementation scope. Per the severity guidance (a returns-None-only-when-caching bug = high/critical; a naming nit = low), a forwardable-but-omitted optional field with no incorrect behavior sits at low, not medium. The finding's recommendation (intentionally decide: add assign_auto to update across protocol/client/tool with a test, or document it as create-only) is reasonable."
      },
      {
        "dimension": "tests",
        "title": "No restricted-queue / read-only coverage for component_update, component_delete, component_get update-path",
        "severityClaimed": "low",
        "severityAdjusted": "low",
        "verdict": "confirmed",
        "file": "tests/mcp/tools/test_component_tools.py",
        "location": "TestComponentUpdate, TestComponentDelete classes",
        "description": "Only component_create has a restricted-queue test (test_restricted_queue_raises_error with client_session_with_limits). component_update and component_delete are NOT tested for queue restrictions. This is largely justified by design: the update/delete tools take only component_id (no queue_id) and do NOT call check_queue_access, so there is no restriction path to test (verified: check_queue_access is invoked only in component_create). However this also means update/delete bypass TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES entirely - a user can update/delete a component in a restricted queue by ID. That access-control gap is out of this dimension's scope but is the root reason the test gap exists. Within test scope: component_get and component_update/delete are also not tested with client_session_read_only except component_get (which has test_gets_component_in_read_only_mode). The write tools' non-registration in read-only mode IS covered indirectly by test_server_creation WRITE_TOOL_NAMES + the read-only registration check.",
        "evidence": "test_component_tools.py: only TestComponentCreate has test_restricted_queue_raises_error using client_session_with_limits. component_write.py: check_queue_access(settings, queue_id) is called only inside component_create; component_update/component_delete have no queue_id parameter and no access check.",
        "recommendation": "If update/delete should respect queue limits (product decision), they need a queue lookup + check_queue_access and corresponding restricted-queue tests. If not, document the intentional gap. At minimum, the asymmetry between create (restricted) and update/delete (unrestricted) should be a conscious, documented choice.",
        "verifyReasoning": "All factual claims in the finding are independently verified against the PR diff (/tmp/pr30.diff) and the existing repo code.

1. Restricted-queue test asymmetry — CONFIRMED. In tests/mcp/tools/test_component_tools.py, only TestComponentCreate has test_restricted_queue_raises_error (diff lines 759-770) using client_session_with_limits. TestComponentUpdate (lines 773-837) and TestComponentDelete (lines 840-856) have no restricted-queue test.

2. check_queue_access only in component_create — CONFIRMED. component_write.py: component_create calls check_queue_access(settings, queue_id) (line 210); component_update (lines 225-252) and component_delete (lines 259-269) take only component_id and perform no access check. So there is literally no restriction code path to test in update/delete — the test gap is justified by design, exactly as the finding states.

3. component_get read-only test exists — CONFIRMED (test_gets_component_in_read_only_mode, lines 674-688). Write-tool non-registration in read-only mode is covered indirectly via test_server_creation WRITE_TOOL_NAMES (diff lines 604-609).

The finding is honest and correctly self-scoped: it explicitly labels the deeper access-control gap (update/delete bypass TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES by ID) as "out of this dimension's scope" rather than overstating it. That underlying gap is real and non-trivial — the project already has check_issue_access (mcp/tools/_access.py) enforcing restriction-by-ID for issues, establishing a precedent that ID-addressed operations CAN be gated. Note the Component model (proto/types/components.py) only carries id/version/name/description and drops the API's `queue` field (extra=\"ignore\"), so enforcing a queue check on update/delete would require also surfacing the queue. But that is a code/security concern, not a test concern.

Severity: the finding claims low, which is accurate for the TESTS dimension — you cannot meaningfully test a restriction path that does not exist, so the missing tests are appropriately low/nit. The recommendation (make the create-vs-update/delete asymmetry a conscious, documented choice, or add queue lookup + check + tests) is sound. Keeping severity at low because, framed as a test-coverage finding, that is the correct weight; the higher-severity access-control implication is flagged as context, not the finding's own claim."
      },
      {
        "dimension": "tests",
        "title": "test_server_creation READ_ONLY/WRITE tool lists are correct (no issue)",
        "severityClaimed": "nit",
        "severityAdjusted": "nit",
        "verdict": "confirmed",
        "file": "tests/mcp/server/test_server_creation.py",

        "description": "Positive finding: the new tool names are registered in the correct lists. component_get is in READ_ONLY_TOOL_NAMES; component_create/component_update/component_delete are in WRITE_TOOL_NAMES. This correctly matches the tool annotations (readOnlyHint True for get, False for the write trio) and the registration gating in tools/__init__.py (component_write registered only when not read_only). The full server-creation test plus all 112 component tests pass.",
        "evidence": "test_server_creation.py diff: READ_ONLY adds `# Component read tools (1)` + `component_get`; WRITE adds component_create/update/delete. tools/__init__.py registers register_component_read_tools unconditionally and register_component_write_tools only inside `if not settings.tracker_read_only`. `uv run pytest ...` -> 112 passed.",
        "recommendation": "No action needed - this part conforms to convention.",
        "verifyReasoning": "Independently reproduced against the PR branch (component-management, commit 892efc0), since main does not contain the PR. The finding is a positive "no issue" claim and it is accurate. In tests/mcp/server/test_server_creation.py (PR version): component_get is in READ_ONLY_TOOL_NAMES (line 30, under comment "# Component read tools (1)"); component_create/component_update/component_delete are in WRITE_TOOL_NAMES (lines 40-42). These placements match the tool annotations: component_read.py declares component_get with ToolAnnotations(readOnlyHint=True), and component_write.py declares all three write tools with ToolAnnotations(readOnlyHint=False). The registration gating in mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/__init__.py (PR version) calls register_component_read_tools unconditionally (line 41) and register_component_write_tools only inside `if not settings.tracker_read_only` (lines 45/48). Tests pass: a fresh worktree on the PR branch ran test_server_creation.py + test_component_tools.py with 99 passed (the "112" figure is a broader component-test count, not the load-bearing claim here; the count assertions use len(READ_ONLY_TOOL_NAMES)/len(WRITE_TOOL_NAMES), not comments, so they pass). Severity correctly remains a nit: it is a positive finding requiring no action. Incidental, not part of this finding: the PR left two stale comments in the same file (header still says "(24 tools)" though the list now has 25 entries, and "# Issue read tools (9)" vs "(10)" on main) — purely cosmetic, no functional impact, and does not change the verdict."
      }
    ],
    "refuted": []
  }

content.text:
I have the full verified picture now — 26 findings, adversarially verified (0 refuted, several severities honestly recalibrated), cross-checked against my own independent reading of the live Yandex Tracker API docs and the codebase. Here's the consolidated review.

---

# Ревью PR #30 — «Queue component management (Create/Get/Update/Delete)»

**Автор:** asanisimov · **Ветка:** `component-management` → `main` · +1132/−1, 18 файлов
**Статус:** ⚠️ `CONFLICTING / DIRTY` — есть конфликты с `main`, мержить в текущем виде нельзя (нужен rebase).

## Краткий вердикт

Архитектурно PR **очень аккуратно встроен** в проект: правильное расслоение proto → client → caching → context → tool, корректное разделение read/write, write-инструменты под `tracker_read_only`, хорошее тестовое покрытие HTTP- и MCP-слоёв, `manifest.json` обновлён. Create-путь **полностью** соответствует API.

Но мержить пока нельзя: есть **два реальных дефекта** (один — баг с кешем, один — обход контроля доступа) и **несколько вопросов соответствия API**, которые надо подтвердить на живом Tracker. Разберу по приоритетам.

---

## 🔴 Блокеры

### 1. `component_get` возвращает `None` при включённом кешировании
`mcp_tracker/tracker/caching/client.py` — `CachingComponentsProtocol`

`CachingComponentsProtocol` переопределяет `component_create/update/delete`, но **не** `component_get`. MRO: `CachingComponentsProtocol → ComponentsProtocolWrap → ComponentsProtocol(Protocol)`. `ComponentsProtocolWrap` определяет только `__init__`, поэтому вызов `component_get` доходит до тела-заглушки протокола `... ` и **возвращает `None`**, ни разу не дёрнув `self._original` и реальный HTTP-клиент.

Воспроизведено в рантайме: при `TOOLS_CACHE_ENABLED=true` `await caching_components.component_get(111175)` → `None`. Инструмент объявляет возврат `Component`, так что FastMCP получает `None` (пустой/битый результат). Сравните с `CachingQueuesProtocol`, где переопределены **все** методы протокола, включая `queue_get`.

```python
# нужно добавить, по аналогии с queue_get:
@cached(**cache_config)
async def component_get(self, component_id: int, *, auth: YandexAuth | None = None) -> Component:
    return await self._original.component_get(component_id, auth=auth)
```
Заодно: `component_get` — read-метод, его надо обернуть `@cached(**cache_config)`, иначе кеша для него не будет вообще. *(severity: high — за опциональным, не-дефолтным флагом, но полностью ломает 1 из 4 инструментов.)*

### 2. Обход `TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES` в get/update/delete
`mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/component_write.py`, `component_read.py`

`component_create` корректно вызывает `check_queue_access(settings, queue_id)`. А `component_update`, `component_delete` и read-инструмент `component_get` принимают только числовой `component_id` и **не делают никакой проверки доступа**. При сконфигурированном `TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES` вызывающий может прочитать, изменить или **безвозвратно удалить** компонент из очереди вне allowlist, просто передав его id. Это нарушение того самого инварианта, который проект соблюдает везде (`check_issue_access` / `check_queue_access` во всех остальных инструментах с одиночным ресурсом).

Чинится не «в лоб», т.к. id не содержит очереди: нужно либо резолвить очередь компонента через `component_get` и проверять `check_queue_access(component.queue.key)` (а для этого — вернуть поле `queue` в модель, см. #4), либо принимать `queue_id` параметром и валидировать. Как минимум — добавить тесты на ограничение для всех трёх инструментов (сейчас он есть только у `create`). *(severity: high.)*

### 3. Конфликты с `main`
`mergeStateStatus = DIRTY`. Нужен rebase/merge `main` и прогон `task` (см. также нит про сортировку импортов ниже, который сейчас **уронит** `task check`).

---

## 🟡 Соответствие API Yandex Tracker (на чём просили сфокусироваться)

Я независимо сверился с живой документацией. Задокументированы только **три** операции с компонентами:
- `GET /v3/components` — **список** всех компонентов организации (не per-queue, не by-id);
- `POST /v3/components` — создание;
- `PATCH /v3/components/<id>?version=<version>` — редактирование.

### 4. `GET /v3/components/{id}` (одиночный) — недокументирован ⚠️
`component_get` бьёт в `GET v3/components/{id}`, а `component_update` **зависит** от него (делает GET → читает `version` → PATCH). В документации одиночного GET нет — есть только LIST `GET /v3/components`. Tracker иногда поддерживает недокументированные by-id GET'ы, так что **возможно, работает**, но PR строит на этом два инструмента без подтверждения.

**Действие:** проверить `GET /v3/components/{id}` на живой организации. Если работает — оставить комментарий в коде. Если нет — `component_get` бэкать списком (`GET /v3/components` + фильтр на клиенте), а `component_update` читать `version` оттуда же или принимать `version` параметром. *(severity: medium — «не работает» не доказано, но риск реальный.)*

### 5. `DELETE /v3/components/{id}` — недокументирован
В API-референсе DELETE для компонентов нет, в пользовательской справке удаление описано только через UI. Падение будет «чистым» (raise_for_status пробросит исключение), без тихих ошибок. **Проверить на живом Tracker**, ожидать 204. *(severity: low.)*

### 6. `component_update` не отправляет `assignAuto`
PATCH-эндпоинт документированно принимает `name`, `description`, `lead`, **`assignAuto`**. `component_create` поддерживает `assign_auto`, а `component_update` — нет, ни на одном из 4 слоёв (tool/protocol/client/caching). Пользователь может включить авто-назначение при создании, но не может выключить/изменить потом. Добавить `assign_auto: bool | None` в `component_update` по всей цепочке. *(severity: medium.)*

### 7. Модель `Component` теряет `queue` / `lead` / `assignAuto` / `self`
`mcp_tracker/tracker/proto/types/components.py` объявляет только `id/version/name/description`. Поскольку `BaseTrackerEntity` — `extra="ignore"`, остальные поля ответа **молча выбрасываются**. Последствие: создав компонент с `lead` и `assign_auto=True`, пользователь в ответе не увидит ни лида, ни флага, ни очереди. Собственные тесты PR это показывают — в ответе есть `lead`/`assignAuto`, но проверяется только `name`. Плюс именно отсутствие `queue` блокирует фикс #2.

```python
queue: QueueReference | None = NoneExcludedField        # refs.py уже есть
lead: UserReference | None = NoneExcludedField           # refs.py уже есть
assignAuto: bool | None = NoneExcludedField
```
*(severity: medium.)* Заодно стоит подумать о том, чтобы сделать `id`/`name` обязательными (как в `QueueVersion`), но это не обязательно.

### 8. Read-then-PATCH: лишний round-trip и сломанная optimistic concurrency
`component_update` всегда делает GET (ради `version`) перед PATCH. Проблемы: (а) зависит от недокументированного одиночного GET (#4); (б) поскольку версия читается прямо перед записью, проверка `?version=` фактически **никогда не сработает** — два конкурентных редактирования молча затрут друг друга (last-write-wins); (в) нет способа передать `version` явно. Прецедент `queue_create_version` read-then-write не делает.

**Действие:** добавить опциональный `version: int | None`; если передан — PATCH напрямую без GET; фолбэк на чтение — только если опущен. *(severity: medium; в MCP-контексте конкуренция редкая, поэтому не high.)*

### 9. Нет инструмента «список компонентов очереди»
Единственный документированный read — это LIST `GET /v3/components`, и он не выставлен. В итоге `get/update/delete` требуют числовой `component_id`, а способа его узнать через инструменты практически нет (только косвенно — через `components` в ответе `issue_get`; `queue_get expand=components` не помогает, т.к. модель `Queue` тоже дропает это поле). Стоит добавить `components_list` / `queue_get_components` поверх `GET /v3/components` с клиентским фильтром по очереди (и `check_queue_access` при фильтре). *(severity: low, но сильно влияет на юзабилити всего набора.)*

✅ **Что по API корректно:** `POST /v3/components` — имена/регистр полей (`name`, `queue`=ключ, `lead`=логин-строка, `assignAuto` camelCase), required-поля, статус 201; `PATCH /v3/components/<id>?version=` — путь и version-параметр. `lead` как строка-логин для компонентов соответствует доке **точнее**, чем общий `UserID`.

---

## 🟡 Архитектура (на чём просили сфокусироваться)

- **Расслоение** — образцовое: protocol/client/caching/context/server/tools/manifest все на месте, write-инструменты под `if not settings.tracker_read_only`. Единственный изъян слоя кеша — пропущенный `component_get` (#1).
- **Контроль доступа** — главный архитектурный провал (#2): нарушен инвариант «каждый одиночный ресурс проходит `check_*_access`».
- **Модель** — недоописана (#7); `ComponentReference` в `refs.py` (лёгкая ссылка) и новый полный `Component` — это разные сущности, конфликта нет, но стоит держать в голове.
- **Именование/консистентность** — в целом по конвенции; `QueueID` переиспользован, аннотации `ToolAnnotations` корректны.

---

## 🟢 Тесты

- **Нет ни одного теста слоя кеша для компонентов** (`tests/tracker/caching/` содержит файлы для queues/issues/users, но не components). Именно поэтому баг #1 проскользнул. `test_protocol_conformance` его бы не поймал — `runtime_checkable` проверяет лишь наличие методов по имени. Нужен поведенческий тест `test_component_get_calls_original`, проверяющий делегирование в `_original` (по образцу `test_queue_get_calls_original`). *(severity: medium.)*
- **`TestComponentGet` лежит в `test_component_update.py`** — нет `test_component_get.py`, ищущий по имени файла не найдёт. *(low)*
- **`test_missing_version_raises`** использует `try/except + assert False` вместо `pytest.raises(ValueError, match=...)` — единственное место в сьюте с таким паттерном. *(low)*
- **`component_update` с пустым телом**: если не передать ни одного поля, уйдёт `PATCH` с `json={}` (плюс лишний GET). Стоит сделать short-circuit. *(low)*
- Нет restricted-queue тестов для update/delete/get — но это следствие отсутствия самих проверок (#2).
- ✅ Списки `READ_ONLY_TOOL_NAMES` / `WRITE_TOOL_NAMES` в `test_server_creation` заполнены **корректно**.

## 🟢 Документация

- **`README.md` и `README_ru.md` не обновлены** для 4 новых инструментов, хотя чеклист в `CLAUDE.md` («Adding New MCP Tools», шаг 6) требует обновить **все три** файла (manifest обновлён, README — нет). *(low, но это нарушение явного правила проекта.)*

## ⚪ Ниты

- **Сортировка импортов в `tests/mcp/conftest.py`**: `from ...proto.components import ComponentsProtocol` вставлен после `issues`, ломая алфавитный порядок. Важно: это правило `I001` (isort) в ruff → `task check` **упадёт**, пока не прогнать `task format`. Остальные хунки PR (`context.py`, `server.py`, `client.py`) порядок соблюдают.
- Устаревшие комментарии в `test_server_creation.py` («(24 tools)», «Issue read tools (9)») — косметика.
- **НЕ менять** `ValueError` на `TrackerError` в `component_update` (одно из findings это предлагало, но при проверке оказалось, что `ValueError` — как раз конвенция этого файла: см. `_build_headers`, `issue_close`). Оставить как есть.

---

## Чеклист перед мержем

**Обязательно:**
1. Добавить делегирующий `@cached` `component_get` в `CachingComponentsProtocol` (#1).
2. Закрыть обход `TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES` в get/update/delete (#2) — вернуть `queue` в модель и проверять доступ.
3. Rebase на `main`, прогнать `task` (исправит и нит с импортами) (#3).
4. Проверить на живом Tracker, что `GET /v3/components/{id}` и `DELETE /v3/components/{id}` существуют (#4, #5) — это фундамент трёх инструментов.

**Желательно:**
5. Добавить `assign_auto` в `component_update` (#6); расширить модель `Component` (#7); добавить опциональный `version` (#8); добавить list-инструмент (#9).
6. Caching-тест на делегирование (#7-тесты); обновить README.md/README_ru.md.

**Мелочи:** вынести `TestComponentGet`; заменить `try/except` на `pytest.raises`; short-circuit пустого update.

Хотите — могу оформить ключевые пункты (#1, #2, #6) как inline-комментарии к PR или сразу подготовить патч с фиксами.

content.text: All anchors located. Now I'll post a single review (event `COMMENT`) carrying the general body (rebase #3 + format/lint/tests note) plus 12 inline comments anchored to exact lines. I'll build the payload in Python to avoid shell-escaping issues with the code blocks.

input.content:
import json
import subprocess

SHA = open("/tmp/pr30_sha.txt").read().strip()
REPO = open("/tmp/pr30_repo.txt").read().strip()

body = """Спасибо за PR — фича полезная, и в целом всё аккуратно встроено в архитектуру: корректное расслоение proto → client → caching → context → tool, разделение read/write, write-инструменты под `tracker_read_only`, `manifest.json` обновлён. Ниже — комментарии по коду.

**Ключевое до мержа:**
1. Ветка сейчас конфликтует с `main` (`CONFLICTING`) — нужно отребейзить на свежий `main` и переразрешить конфликты.
2. Прогнать `task` (format + lint + type-check + tests) перед пушем. Сейчас, например, импорт в `tests/mcp/conftest.py` нарушает порядок isort (правило `I001`), и `task check` на этом упадёт — `task format` это чинит. После rebase давай убедимся, что весь набор проходит.

Содержательные пункты — в инлайн-комментариях ниже: баг с кешем у `component_get`, обход `TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES` в get/update/delete, вопросы по недокументированным эндпоинтам, недостающие поля модели, параметры `version`/`assignAuto` и недостающие тесты."""

comments = [
    # --- #1 caching None bug ---
    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/tracker/caching/client.py",
        "line": 479,
        "side": "RIGHT",
        "body": """🔴 Баг: при включённом кешировании (`TOOLS_CACHE_ENABLED=true`) `component_get` будет возвращать `None`.

`CachingComponentsProtocol` переопределяет `component_create/update/delete`, но не `component_get`. По MRO вызов уходит в `ComponentsProtocol.component_get`, тело которого — заглушка `...`, т.е. метод возвращает `None`, не обращаясь к `self._original` (воспроизводится в рантайме). Сравните с `CachingQueuesProtocol`, где переопределены все методы протокола.

Нужен делегирующий override, и, как у остальных read-методов, обёрнутый `@cached`:
```python
@cached(**cache_config)
async def component_get(self, component_id: int, *, auth: YandexAuth | None = None) -> Component:
    return await self._original.component_get(component_id, auth=auth)
```""",
    },
    # --- #2 access control bypass (write tools) ---
    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/component_write.py",
        "line": 64,
        "side": "RIGHT",
        "body": """🔴 Обход `TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES`. `component_create` корректно делает `check_queue_access(settings, queue_id)`, а `component_update` (здесь), `component_delete` (ниже) и read-инструмент `component_get` принимают только числовой `component_id` и доступ не проверяют. При сконфигурированном allowlist это позволяет прочитать / изменить / **безвозвратно удалить** компонент из очереди вне списка по одному только id.

Чинится не «в лоб» (id не содержит очереди): нужно вернуть поле `queue` в модель `Component` (см. отдельный комментарий) и резолвить очередь через `component_get` → `check_queue_access(component.queue.key)`, либо принимать `queue_id` параметром. Плюс restricted-queue тесты на все три инструмента (сейчас он есть только у create).""",
    },
    # --- #2b read-side cross-ref ---
    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/component_read.py",
        "line": 24,
        "side": "RIGHT",
        "body": """Сюда тоже относится обход `TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES` (подробности — в комментарии к `component_update`): `component_get` отдаёт компонент по id без проверки доступа к его очереди.""",
    },
    # --- #4 undocumented GET /{id} ---
    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/tracker/custom/client.py",
        "line": 380,
        "side": "RIGHT",
        "body": """❓ Вопрос по API: эндпоинт `GET /v3/components/{id}` (получение одного компонента по id) в документации Яндекс Трекера я не нашёл — задокументированы только список `GET /v3/components`, создание `POST /v3/components` и редактирование `PATCH /v3/components/{id}?version=`. На этот `component_get` к тому же опирается `component_update` (берёт оттуда `version`).

Странно, что документации нет — он точно работает на живой организации? Если да — давай оставим в коде пометку, что эндпоинт недокументированный. Если нет — `component_get` придётся бэкать списком (`GET /v3/components` + фильтр по id), а версию для update брать оттуда же / из явного параметра.""",
    },
    # --- #5 undocumented DELETE ---
    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/tracker/custom/client.py",
        "line": 427,
        "side": "RIGHT",
        "body": """❓ Аналогичный вопрос: `DELETE /v3/components/{id}` тоже не задокументирован — в API-референсе для компонентов есть только list / create / edit, а в пользовательской справке удаление описано лишь через UI. Точно ли DELETE работает по API (ожидаем 204)? Если да — добавь, пожалуйста, пометку в коде, что эндпоинт недокументированный.""",
    },
    # --- #6 assignAuto missing in update ---
    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/component_write.py",
        "line": 78,
        "side": "RIGHT",
        "body": """PATCH-эндпоинт документированно принимает `assignAuto`, и `component_create` его поддерживает, а `component_update` — нет (ни здесь, ни в protocol / client / caching). Получается, авто-назначение можно включить при создании, но нельзя изменить потом. Давай добавим `assign_auto: bool | None` в update по всей цепочке (tool → protocol → client → caching) и тест.""",
    },
    # --- #7 model drops fields ---
    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/tracker/proto/types/components.py",
        "line": 10,
        "side": "RIGHT",
        "body": """Модель `Component` описывает только `id/version/name/description`, а API возвращает ещё `queue`, `lead`, `assignAuto` (и `self`). Из-за `extra="ignore"` в `BaseTrackerEntity` они молча отбрасываются — после create/update пользователь не увидит ни выставленный `lead`/`assignAuto`, ни очередь. Отсутствие `queue` к тому же блокирует фикс контроля доступа.

Предлагаю добавить (типы уже есть в `refs.py`):
```python
queue: QueueReference | None = NoneExcludedField
lead: UserReference | None = NoneExcludedField
assignAuto: bool | None = NoneExcludedField
```""",
    },
    # --- #8 version handling ---
    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/tracker/custom/client.py",
        "line": 402,
        "side": "RIGHT",
        "body": """По-хорошему `version` для оптимистичной блокировки должен передавать сам агент — в этом и смысл механизма (защита от конкурентного изменения): клиент знает, какую версию он редактирует. Сейчас же мы всегда делаем GET прямо перед PATCH и берём самую свежую версию, из-за чего проверка `?version=` фактически никогда не срабатывает и два параллельных редактирования молча затирают друг друга.

Давай добавим опциональный `version: int | None` (tool → protocol → client): если передан — PATCH сразу с ним, без GET; если не передан — фолбэк на текущее поведение (читаем версию через `component_get`). Заодно уберём лишний round-trip в типичном случае.""",
    },
    # --- #9 list-all-org-components tool ---
    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/component_read.py",
        "line": 16,
        "side": "RIGHT",
        "body": """Стоит добавить инструмент для получения всех компонентов организации поверх документированного `GET /v3/components` — это единственный задокументированный read-эндпоинт и естественный способ узнать id компонентов (без него get/update/delete по id неудобно использовать). Опционально — с клиентским фильтром по очереди и `check_queue_access` при его передаче.""",
    },
    # --- tests: split files ---
    {
        "path": "tests/tracker/custom/components/test_component_update.py",

import subprocess

SHA = open("/tmp/pr30_sha.txt").read().strip()
REPO = open("/tmp/pr30_repo.txt").read().strip()

body = """Спасибо за PR — фича полезная, и в целом всё аккуратно встроено в архитектуру: корректное расслоение proto → client → caching → context → tool, разделение read/write, write-инструменты под `tracker_read_only`, `manifest.json` обновлён. Ниже — комментарии по коду.

**Ключевое до мержа:**
1. Ветка сейчас конфликтует с `main` (`CONFLICTING`) — нужно отребейзить на свежий `main` и переразрешить конфликты.
2. Прогнать `task` (format + lint + type-check + tests) перед пушем. Сейчас, например, импорт в `tests/mcp/conftest.py` нарушает порядок isort (правило `I001`), и `task check` на этом упадёт — `task format` это чинит. После rebase давай убедимся, что весь набор проходит.

Содержательные пункты — в инлайн-комментариях ниже: баг с кешем у `component_get`, обход `TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES` в get/update/delete, вопросы по недокументированным эндпоинтам, недостающие поля модели, параметры `version`/`assignAuto` и недостающие тесты."""

comments = [
    # --- #1 caching None bug ---
    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/tracker/caching/client.py",
        "line": 479,
        "side": "RIGHT",
        "body": """🔴 Баг: при включённом кешировании (`TOOLS_CACHE_ENABLED=true`) `component_get` будет возвращать `None`.

`CachingComponentsProtocol` переопределяет `component_create/update/delete`, но не `component_get`. По MRO вызов уходит в `ComponentsProtocol.component_get`, тело которого — заглушка `...`, т.е. метод возвращает `None`, не обращаясь к `self._original` (воспроизводится в рантайме). Сравните с `CachingQueuesProtocol`, где переопределены все методы протокола.

Нужен делегирующий override, и, как у остальных read-методов, обёрнутый `@cached`:
```python
@cached(**cache_config)
async def component_get(self, component_id: int, *, auth: YandexAuth | None = None) -> Component:
    return await self._original.component_get(component_id, auth=auth)
```""",
    },
    # --- #2 access control bypass (write tools) ---
    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/component_write.py",
        "line": 64,
        "side": "RIGHT",
        "body": """🔴 Обход `TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES`. `component_create` корректно делает `check_queue_access(settings, queue_id)`, а `component_update` (здесь), `component_delete` (ниже) и read-инструмент `component_get` принимают только числовой `component_id` и доступ не проверяют. При сконфигурированном allowlist это позволяет прочитать / изменить / **безвозвратно удалить** компонент из очереди вне списка по одному только id.

Чинится не «в лоб» (id не содержит очереди): нужно вернуть поле `queue` в модель `Component` (см. отдельный комментарий) и резолвить очередь через `component_get` → `check_queue_access(component.queue.key)`, либо принимать `queue_id` параметром. Плюс restricted-queue тесты на все три инструмента (сейчас он есть только у create).""",
    },
    # --- #2b read-side cross-ref ---
    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/component_read.py",

    },

На этот `component_get` к тому же опирается `component_update` (берёт оттуда `version`).

    },

    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/mcp/tools/component_write.py",
        "line": 78,
        "side": "RIGHT",
        "body": """PATCH-эндпоинт документированно принимает `assignAuto`, и `component_create` его поддерживает, а `component_update` — нет (ни здесь, ни в protocol / client / caching). Получается, авто-назначение можно включить при создании, но нельзя изменить потом. Давай добавим `assign_auto: bool | None` в update по всей цепочке (tool → protocol → client → caching) и тест.""",

Из-за `extra="ignore"` в `BaseTrackerEntity` они молча отбрасываются — после create/update пользователь не увидит ни выставленный `lead`/`assignAuto`, ни очередь. Отсутствие `queue` к тому же блокирует фикс контроля доступа.

Предлагаю добавить (типы уже есть в `refs.py`):
```python
queue: QueueReference | None = NoneExcludedField
lead: UserReference | None = NoneExcludedField
assignAuto: bool | None = NoneExcludedField
```""",
    },
    # --- #8 version handling ---
    {
        "path": "mcp_tracker/tracker/custom/client.py",
        "line": 402,
        "side": "RIGHT",
        "body": """По-хорошему `version` для оптимистичной блокировки должен передавать сам агент — в этом и смысл механизма (защита от конкурентного изменения): клиент знает, какую версию он редактирует. Сейчас же мы всегда делаем GET прямо перед PATCH и берём самую свежую версию, из-за чего проверка `?version=` фактически никогда не срабатывает и два параллельных редактирования молча затирают друг друга.

Давай добавим опциональный `version: int | None` (tool → protocol → client): если передан — PATCH сразу с ним, без GET; если не передан — фолбэк на текущее поведение (читаем версию через `component_get`). Заодно уберём лишний round-trip в типичном случае.""",

    {

Опционально — с клиентским фильтром по очереди и `check_queue_access` при его передаче.""",

    {

Готово. Ревью опубликовано на PR #30 как single review (event `COMMENT`) с общим телом и **13 инлайн-комментариями**, привязанными к нужным строкам.

🔗 https://github.com/aikts/yandex-tracker-mcp/pull/30#pullrequestreview-4530969046

**В теле ревью** (пункт 3 + прогон тулинга):
- ребейз на `main` (ветка `CONFLICTING`);
- прогнать `task` (format/lint/type-check/tests) перед пушем, с конкретным примером падения по `I001`.

**Инлайн-комментарии:**

| Пункт | Файл:строка | Суть |
|---|---|---|
| 1 🔴 | `caching/client.py:479` | `component_get` возвращает `None` при кеше — добавить `@cached`-override |
| 2 🔴 | `component_write.py:64` | обход `TRACKER_LIMIT_QUEUES` в update/delete/get |
| 2 | `component_read.py:24` | кросс-ссылка на обход доступа в `component_get` |
| 4 ❓ | `custom/client.py:380` | `GET /v3/components/{id}` недокументирован — точно работает? |
| 5 ❓ | `custom/client.py:427` | `DELETE /v3/components/{id}` недокументирован (в справке — только UI) |
| 6 | `component_write.py:78` | `assignAuto` есть в create, нет в update |
| 7 | `proto/types/components.py:10` | модель теряет `queue`/`lead`/`assignAuto` |
| 8 | `custom/client.py:402` | `version` должен передавать агент, GET — как фолбэк |
| 9 | `component_read.py:16` | добавить метод получения всех компонентов организации |
| тесты | `test_component_update.py:10` | разнести тесты по файлам (`test_component_get.py`) |
| тесты | `test_component_update.py:315` | `pytest.raises` вместо `try/except + assert False` |
| тесты | `test_component_tools.py:114` | нет тестов кеша и restricted-queue для update/delete/get |
| линт | `conftest.py:18` | нарушен порядок isort (`I001`) — пример для пункта про `task` |

Все комментарии оставлены под аккаунтом `igorcoding`. Для пунктов 4 и 5 формулировки сделаны как вопросы (с просьбой подтвердить работу эндпоинтов на живой организации и оставить пометку в коде), для пункта 8 учтена твоя ремарка про передачу версии агентом с фолбэком на GET.
